title: Operating a contextual Stern-Gerlach apparatus
abstract: We propose a contextual cavity/circuit QED analogue and extension of the Stern-Gerlach experiment, where the pseudo-spin of a two-state `atomic' transition plays the role of the ``spin'', while the resonant field driving the transition stands for the ``magnetic field''. A phase-sensitive continuous detection of the cavity field coupled to the induced `atomic' dipole affects the stability of the two distinct outcomes. The dressed states comprising the latter give their place to a self-consistent spontaneous dressed-state polarization as the driving strength is lowered. The associated evolution proves anew highly contextual, underpinned by a persistent production of coherent-state superpositions for a particular setting of the monitoring device. Finally, when bistability is absent, we employ the photoelectron `atomic' emission statistics as a diagnostic tool of the cavity field fluctuations.
summary: This paper proposes a quantum optical version of the Stern-Gerlach experiment using a cavity QED platform. It shows that the way a system is monitored (e.g., via homodyne or heterodyne detection) changes the physical outcomes, demonstrating quantum contextuality. The research highlights how measurement backaction can drive the system into spontaneous polarization or create transient Schrödinger cat-like states. Additionally, it explores how photoelectron statistics can reveal squeezed fluctuations in the bad-cavity limit.
main_problem: The paper proposes and investigates a cavity/circuit QED analogue of the Stern-Gerlach experiment to explore how quantum contextuality and measurement backaction affect the stability of dressed states.
main_result: The study demonstrates that different measurement unravellings (homodyne vs. heterodyne) lead to non-equivalent physical outcomes, such as biased vs. symmetric dressed-state populations, and identifies the emergence of spontaneous dressed-state polarization and coherent-state superpositions.
method: The author uses quantum trajectory theory, specifically Monte Carlo wave-function simulations and the Stochastic Schrödinger Equation, to unravel the Lindblad master equation.
model_or_system: A Jaynes-Cummings system consisting of a two-state atom coupled to a cavity mode, driven by a resonant field, and subject to cavity photon loss and spontaneous emission.
key_observables: Quadrature phase operators, measurement signals (homodyne/heterodyne), Wigner functions, Bloch-sphere trajectories, and photoelectron waiting-time distributions.
important_parameters: Driving strength (epsilon), coupling strength (g), cavity decay (kappa), atomic decay (gamma), and the local oscillator phase (theta).
main_assumptions: The study utilizes the rotating wave approximation (RWA), the secular approximation, and considers the 'zero system size' limit (gamma/2kappa = 0) in certain regimes.
figures_summary: Figure 3 depicts Bloch-sphere trajectories and conditioned Wigner functions showing the evolution of the system.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a cavity QED analogue of the Stern-Gerlach experiment, defines the Hamiltonian and master equation, explores different measurement unravellings (homodyne and heterodyne), analyzes the effects of local oscillator phase on state stability, and concludes with the use of photoelectron statistics as a diagnostic tool.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into how the choice of measurement scheme (unravelling) fundamentally alters the observed steady-state dynamics and symmetry breaking in open quantum systems, which is central to quantum optics and the study of quantum contextuality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the Stern–Gerlach appara- tus. Fluorescence from a resonantly driven two-state ‘atom’ is superimposed with a strong local oscillator (LO) in a bal- anced detection scheme. A cavity with photon loss rate 2κ collects forward radiation into a resonant mode of frequency ω, and directs it out one of its mirrors. The measurement signal iθ(t) (homodyne scheme, ω′ = ω and fixed θ) or i(t) [heterodyne scheme, ω′ −ω ≫κ and θ = (ω −ω′)t] arises from the detection of two fields with a π phase difference be- tween their superpositions. Simultaneously, cavity-inhibited spontaneous emission is collected from detectors situated at the sides of the cavity. The conditional...

FIG. 2. Contextual stability of stationary states. Sample trajectories [solutions to Eq. (4)] in the Bloch-sphere representation (X, Y, Z), with X −iY = 2⟨ψREC(t)|σ−|ψREC(t)⟩and Z = ⟨ψREC(t)|σz|ψREC(t)⟩, in the course of 100 cavity lifetimes. The green square and the red circle mark the start and end of the trajectory, respectively. The LO phase is set to θ = 0 in frames (a, b), π/3 in (c) and 2π/3 in (d). The upper side panels in (a, b) depict the conditioned Wigner function of the cavity field WREC(x + iy) at κt = 60. The lower ones plot X(t), as do the side panels of (c, d). The bottom left surface plot depicts the steady-state Wigner function of the resonant cavity mode, Wss(x + iy)....

FIG. 3. Spontaneous dressed-state polarization vs. persistence of coherent-state superpositions. The central panel depicts a sam- ple trajectory in the Bloch-sphere representation (X, Y, Z), in a similar fashion to Fig. 2, with the LO phase set to θ = π/2. The surrounding conditioned Wigner functions WREC(t) are extracted for the times κ(t1, t2, . . . , t5) = 15.200, 23.340, 54.890, 78.015 and 84.570, indicated by the dashed vertical lines in the corresponding trajectory for Y (t). The schematic surface plot be- tween the frames t2 and t3 depicts the Wigner function corresponding to the (appropriately normalized) superposition state |αB+⟩+ eiϕ|αB−⟩with ϕ = 0. In the framed group of panels...

FIG. 4. Photoelectron statistics in the bad-cavity limit under heterodyne detection. Histogram of waiting times for the pho- toelectric emissions to the sides of the cavity (extracted from an individual trajectory along 104 cavity lifetimes) on top of the waiting-time distribution, w(τ), for g/γ = 0.1 in (a) and 1 in (b). The above exclusive probability density function is calculated using the steady-state solution of the ME (1) and the quantum regression formula. The two insets depict the corresponding steady-state Wigner function Wss(x + iy) for the cavity field. Operating parameters read: γ/κ = 5, ε/κ = 20.
